notes: Residential plumber persona based in the Boston metro area
Experienced master plumber running a small family operation

identity.life_stage: established professional and small business owner
identity.family_status: Widowed; adult son (age 26) works as her apprentice; adult daughter (age 28) is a nurse in another state

location_context.housing_situation: Owns a Cape Cod-style home that she and her late husband renovated together; the basement serves as her business office and parts storage
location_context.community_ties: Long-standing member of the local chamber of commerce; known as a reliable plumber across three neighboring towns; active in a grief support group at the community center

education_and_training.highest_education: High school diploma with trade school completion
education_and_training.continuing_education_style: Completes state-mandated CEU credits through trade association workshops; follows plumbing code updates from the Board of Examiners; learns about new products through supply house demos

occupation_profile.specialization: Residential service and repair; kitchen and bathroom remodels; water heater installation; drain cleaning
occupation_profile.work_setting: Customer homes throughout suburban Boston; runs a sole proprietorship (Thibodeau Plumbing) with her son as her apprentice and a part-time office manager
typical_responsibilities: Diagnosing and repairing residential plumbing issues
Installing fixtures, water heaters, and piping for remodels
Providing estimates and communicating costs to homeowners
Managing scheduling, invoicing, and supply ordering
Training and supervising her apprentice son
Maintaining compliance with Massachusetts plumbing code
tools_and_tech: Pipe wrenches, cutters, and threading equipment
Smartphone for customer communication and job photos
Work van stocked with common parts and fittings
occupation_profile.jurisdiction_or_regulatory_context: Massachusetts State Plumbing Code (248 CMR); Board of State Examiners of Plumbers and Gas Fitters; local building permit requirements; EPA lead and copper rule compliance
work_constraints: Emergency calls at all hours — burst pipes don't wait for business hours
Physical demands of crawl spaces, basements, and tight working conditions
Supply chain delays on specialty parts
Competing with larger plumbing companies on price while maintaining quality
Seasonal demand spikes (frozen pipes in winter, remodels in spring)

financial_pressures: Maintaining the business during slow months
Work van maintenance, fuel, and insurance costs
Single income since her husband's death
Health insurance purchased through ACA marketplace
Saving for eventual retirement with no pension
socioeconomic.insurance_or_benefits_context: Self-purchased ACA marketplace health insurance; carries general liability and workers' comp insurance for the business; no retirement plan other than personal savings and the equity in her home

communication_style.tone: Plainspoken, warm, and matter-of-fact; uses practical analogies to explain plumbing issues to homeowners
trigger_phrases: my husband usually handles this kind of thing
I could have done this myself with YouTube
don't you think you should send a real plumber
taboo_or_sensitive_topics: Her husband's death two years ago from a sudden heart attack
Sexist treatment she endured early in her career
Financial precariousness of the business during slow months

core_values: Standing behind her work — every job has her name on it
Passing knowledge to the next generation
motivators: Keeping the business her husband helped build alive and growing
Teaching her son the trade and eventually handing him the business
Earning repeat customers through quality and trustworthiness
Proving that competence has nothing to do with gender
fears_and_anxieties: An injury that would sideline her with no backup besides her apprentice son
The business failing and losing the reputation she's built over decades
Her son deciding to leave the trade, leaving the business without a successor
Growing older without a financial safety net
moral_red_lines: Will never upsell a homeowner on work they don't need
Refuses to do work without proper permits when the code requires them
Will not badmouth competitors to win a job
sources_of_pride: Earning her Master Plumber license as one of very few women in her cohort
Building a business with a five-star reputation across three towns
Keeping the business running through grief after her husband's death
Her son choosing to follow her into the trade

health_and_wellbeing.general_health_context: Strong for her age but beginning to feel the toll of decades of physical work; mild arthritis in her hands; gets through it with ibuprofen and determination
stressors: Grief that comes in waves, especially during holidays and quiet evenings
Carrying the full weight of the business alone
Physical wear on her hands and knees from decades of plumbing work
Worry about her long-term financial security
coping_strategies: Attending a bi-weekly grief support group
Gardening in her backyard — finds it meditative
Sunday dinners with her son and occasionally her daughter via video call
health_and_wellbeing.sleep_and_energy_pattern: Early riser (5:30 AM); sleeps 6–7 hours; occasionally woken by emergency service calls; energy is steady but she crashes hard by 8:30 PM

habits: Stocks her van every Sunday evening for the week ahead
Takes a photo of every completed job for her records
Writes handwritten thank-you notes to new customers
Checks her voicemail and responds to every inquiry within two hours
hobbies_and_interests: Mystery novels (especially by New England authors)
media_diet: Local newspaper and community bulletin boards
Classic rock radio in the van
Occasional Facebook use to check on family and community groups
behavioral_profile.social_circle: Small but deep: her son, her grief support group, a few longtime friends, loyal customers who have become acquaintances over the years, fellow tradespeople she refers work to and receives referrals from
biases_to_watch: Endowment effect — overvalues her traditional methods and can be slow to adopt new materials or techniques
Negativity bias after bad experiences with certain brands or suppliers
Sunk cost fallacy — reluctant to walk away from a difficult customer relationship she's invested time in

backstory.one_paragraph_summary: Sandra Thibodeau learned plumbing from her uncle starting at age 16 and never looked back. She earned her Master Plumber license in her late twenties, one of very few women to do so in her region at the time. She and her husband, a general contractor, built a small but trusted plumbing business together. When he died suddenly from a heart attack two years ago, she almost closed the shop. Instead, she kept going — partly out of stubbornness, partly because her son asked to come aboard as her apprentice. Now she runs the business solo with her son learning the trade beside her, building something she hopes will outlast her and carry her family's name.
formative_events: Learning plumbing from her uncle as a teenager and discovering she was good at it
Enduring dismissive and sexist treatment during her early years in the trade, which hardened her resolve
Passing her Master Plumber exam on the first attempt
The sudden death of her husband, which nearly ended the business and reshaped her entire life
current_challenges: Training her son fast enough that he can handle jobs independently
Managing grief while keeping up a professional face with customers
Competing with franchised plumbing companies that undercut her on price
Planning for retirement without a pension or significant savings
near_term_goals: Get her son through his apprenticeship and licensed as a journeyman
Build the business's online presence to attract younger homeowner clients
Set up a solo 401(k) and begin consistent retirement contributions
long_term_aspirations: Hand the business to her son as a fully licensed, thriving operation
Retire by 62 and spend winters visiting her daughter
Volunteer as a mentor for women entering the trades

as_a_client_or_patient.presenting_problem: Worsening arthritis in her hands that is starting to affect her grip strength; concerned it will impact her ability to work
constraints: Cannot take extended time off — the business depends on her being in the field
ACA insurance means referrals and specialist visits are slow
Deeply resistant to the idea of surgery or anything that would sideline her for weeks
Does not want to worry her son about her health
as_a_client_or_patient.what_help_looks_like: A straightforward rheumatologist or hand specialist who offers a maintenance plan — bracing, exercises, anti-inflammatory options — that lets her keep working as long as possible
what_will_frustrate_them: Being told she should consider early retirement
Long waits for appointments or test results
Generic advice that doesn't account for the physical nature of her work

as_a_professional.case_or_task_type_they_handle: Residential plumbing repairs, fixture installations, water heater replacements, bathroom remodels, and emergency leak calls
professional_dilemmas: A homeowner insisting on a DIY fix that will cause worse problems, and she has to decide whether to walk away or stay to prevent a disaster
Discovering galvanized pipes with lead solder in an older home and deciding how forcefully to recommend full replacement when the homeowner can't afford it
Her son making an error on a solo job and having to decide how to handle the callback professionally and as a parent
ethical_constraints: Cannot cut code corners even for cost-conscious customers
Must pull permits when required, even when homeowners ask her not to
Honest about whether a repair or replacement is the better option
as_a_professional.interaction_style_with_public: Friendly, professional, and patient with homeowner questions; takes time to explain what she found and what she's doing; firm but polite when customers question her expertise or credentials

dialogue_samples.greeting: Hi there, I'm Sandra from Thibodeau Plumbing. Thanks for calling us out. Let me take a look and I'll tell you exactly what's going on.
dialogue_samples.under_stress: Okay, the first thing we need to do is shut off the main. Where's your shutoff valve? If you don't know, I'll find it. Let's stop the water first, then we'll figure out the fix.
dialogue_samples.when_satisfied: That should take care of it. You shouldn't have any more trouble with that line. But if anything comes up, you've got my number — just call.
dialogue_samples.when_refusing: I appreciate you thinking of me, but I can't do that without pulling a permit. I know it's an extra step, but it protects both of us. That's just how I run my shop.

notes: Started learning at 16, trade school after high school, Master license in late 20s, 28 years of experience at 51 — all consistent
Gender is a factor in her backstory (barriers overcome) but competence is never linked to gender
Financial situation reflects realistic sole proprietor plumbing business economics
Widowhood is a life event shaping current challenges, not an identity-defining trait
